2PxFftgAAAAJ.keywords: Information Visualization, Visual Analytics, Human-Computer Interaction, Algorithm Visualization, Software Visualization, Interaction Design for Visualization, Visualization Evaluation, Sensemaking, Natural Language Interfaces for Visualization, Multivariate Data Visualization
2PxFftgAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
John Stasko is **Professor Emeritus of Interactive Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where his long career has been centered in the intersection of **Information Visualization**, **Visual Analytics**, and **Human–Computer Interaction**. He led and collaborated with research groups studying how people interact with visual representations of data and how visual tools can amplify analytic reasoning. His work blends systems and empirical methods to address practical and theoretical problems — from <u>fault localization</u> in software (e.g., visualization of test information) and <u>algorithm animation</u> frameworks (e.g., Tango) to the study of <u>analytic interaction</u> and the generation of <u>insight</u> — with an emphasis on the role of *interaction*, *evaluation*, and *usability* in visualization systems.

---

## Research Areas
Stasko’s research spans core topics in information visualization and its applications. He has made foundational contributions to the design and evaluation of visual representations and interactive techniques (e.g., radial space‑filling displays, focus+context methods) and to understanding how interaction supports analytic activity (see "Toward a deeper understanding of the role of interaction in information visualization" (2007) and "Low‑level components of analytic activity in information visualization" (2005)). He has advanced the use of visualization for software engineering problems, especially debugging and fault localization ("Visualization of test information to assist fault localization" (2002); "Visualization for fault localization" (2001)), and has been an active developer of visualization systems and frameworks (Tango (1990) and later systems such as Jigsaw (2008) and DataMeadow (2008)). His work also addresses pedagogical and cognitive dimensions of visualization — algorithm animation and learning ("A meta‑study of algorithm visualization effectiveness" (2002); XTANGO/Tango work) — as well as newer themes like natural‑language interfaces for visual analysis (NL4DV (2020)), automated narrative/fact generation for charts ("Augmenting visualizations with interactive data facts" (2018)), casual and ambient information displays, and evaluation methodologies that go beyond raw task performance to measure *value*, *insight*, and *user experience*.

---

## Notable Works
- "Visualization of test information to assist fault localization" (2002) — introduced visualization techniques to help developers locate software faults by surfacing suspicious statements and test information, influencing work at the intersection of software engineering and visualization.  
- "Toward a deeper understanding of the role of interaction in information visualization" (2007) — provided conceptual frameworks and taxonomies that shaped how the community thinks about interaction as a first‑class component of InfoVis systems and studies.  
- "Tango: A framework and system for algorithm animation" (1990) — an early and influential framework for algorithm animation that advanced research and teaching practices in algorithm visualization and helped define a research agenda on dynamic visual representations.

---

## Academic Background
John Stasko has had a multi‑decade academic career at the Georgia Institute of Technology in the School of Interactive Computing / College of Computing, advancing research, systems, and evaluation practices in information visualization and human–computer interaction. Over his career he produced a wide portfolio of influential papers, systems, and edited volumes, supervised generations of students, and contributed extensively to the visualization research community through program and editorial service. His scholarship has been widely cited (total citations ~29,225) and spans early work on algorithm animation and program visualization through contemporary contributions to visual analytics, natural‑language interfaces, and visualization evaluation. More information about his publications, projects, and students is available on his professional homepage: https://faculty.cc.gatech.edu/~stasko

2QsddMIAAAAJ.keywords: Materials discovery, Materials informatics, Computational Materials Science, AI for Science, Heterogeneous catalysis, Single-atom catalysts, Metal nanoclusters, Density functional theory, Graph neural networks, Methane activation
2QsddMIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Victor Fung is an **Assistant Professor** in **Computational Science and Engineering** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where he leads the **Fung Group** (https://www.fung-group.org/). His lab combines first-principles electronic-structure methods, atomistic modeling, and modern machine learning to accelerate the design of functional materials. Fung's primary research focus lies at the intersection of **heterogeneous catalysis**, **nanomaterials**, and **materials informatics** — with particular emphasis on <u>single-atom and atomically precise catalysts</u>, <u>structure–property relationships in metal nanoclusters and perovskites</u>, and <u>AI-driven materials discovery</u>. His work spans detailed mechanistic studies of catalytic systems and the development of predictive, physically informed machine learning models that enable high-throughput screening and inverse design.

---

## Research Areas
Fung’s research portfolio integrates computational catalysis and materials machine learning. On the catalysis side, he has advanced understanding of atomically dispersed metals and atomically precise nanoclusters — for example in studies such as "Golden single-atomic-site platinum electrocatalysts" (2018) and "Single rhodium atoms anchored in micropores for efficient transformation of methane under mild conditions" (2018) where the interplay between local geometry, electronic structure, and catalytic function is elucidated. His group has applied density functional theory (DFT) and mechanistic modeling to perovskite and oxide supports ("Exploring perovskites for methane activation from first principles", 2018; "Perovskite-supported Pt single atoms for methane activation", 2020), addressing issues of stabilization, support–metal interactions and reaction selectivity. In nanochemistry, Fung has contributed to atom-by-atom growth and fluxionality studies ("Understanding seed-mediated growth of gold nanoclusters at molecular level", 2017; "Exploring Structural Diversity and Fluxionality of Ptn (n = 10–13) Clusters from First-Principles", 2017), connecting synthesis routes to emergent properties. On the data-driven side, he has helped bridge physics-based descriptors and machine learning — notable works include "Benchmarking graph neural networks for materials chemistry" (2021), "Machine learned features from density of states for accurate adsorption energy prediction" (2021), and more recent efforts in invertible networks and LLM-enabled materials design ("Inverse design of two-dimensional materials with invertible neural networks", 2021; "Llmatdesign: Autonomous materials discovery with large language models", 2024). These efforts emphasize the importance of <u>physically informed features</u>, benchmarking of model architectures, and strategies for scalable, high-throughput property prediction.

---

## Notable Works
- "Golden single-atomic-site platinum electrocatalysts" (2018) — Demonstrated colloidal strategies and atomic-level control of bimetallic sites to tune geometric and electronic effects in electrocatalysts, offering a model platform for correlating site structure with catalytic performance.
- "Insights into interfaces, stability, electronic properties, and catalytic activities of atomically precise metal nanoclusters from first principles" (2018) — Provided a comprehensive first-principles framework for understanding ligand-protected nanoclusters, linking atomic precision to electronic structure and catalytic trends.
- "Benchmarking graph neural networks for materials chemistry" (2021) — Systematically evaluated GNN architectures for materials applications, establishing performance baselines and best practices that have guided subsequent ML-driven materials screening and model selection.

---

## Academic Background
Victor Fung leads an interdisciplinary research program that bridges *computational materials science*, *chemistry*, and *artificial intelligence for science*. His publication record (total citations ~6,141) reflects sustained, high-impact contributions across catalysis, nanomaterials, and materials informatics. At Georgia Tech he heads the Fung Group in Computational Science and Engineering and collaborates broadly with experimental and theoretical teams in catalysis, surface science, and machine learning. His research has been published in high-profile journals and has influenced both fundamental understanding (e.g., single-atom catalysis, nanocluster dynamics, perovskite supports) and methodological advances (physically informed ML features, GNN benchmarking, inverse design). Fung’s work positions him as a leading investigator in the use of computational and data-driven approaches to accelerate materials discovery and to translate atomic-scale insight into design principles for next-generation catalysts and functional materials.
